title: Development and analysis of structure-preserving finite element methods on non-conforming meshes
author: Frederik Schnack
bibtex:
@phdthesis{dissertation,
	author = {Schnack, Frederik Günter},
	title = {Development and analysis of structure-preserving finite element methods on non-conforming meshes},
	year = {2025},
	school = {Technische Universität München},
	pages = {161},
	language = {en},
	abstract = {This work develops structure-preserving finite element methods on two-dimensional refined multipatch spaces. Within the broken-FEEC framework, we construct bounded commuting projections on non-matching discretizations with certain assumptions on the geometry. 
We also design conforming projections, based on interface-averaging and extension-restriction operators, enabling spurious-free numerical schemes.  Additionally, strong-strong discretizations are extended to multipatch setups.},
	keywords = {},
	note = {},
	url = {https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/1776749},
	doi = {},
}

title: Development and implementation of algorithms for kinetic Vlasov systems on various hardware architectures
author: Nils Schild
bibtex:
@phdthesis{dissertation,
	author = {Schild, Nils},
	title = {Development and implementation of algorithms for kinetic Vlasov systems on various hardware architectures},
	year = {2025},
	school = {Technische Universität München},
	pages = {123},
	language = {en},
	abstract = {This thesis examines simulations of strongly magnetized plasmas, focusing on efficient algorithms for the kinetic Vlasov equation. It develops a performance-portable semi-Lagrangian solver with a rotating velocity grid, outperforming standard methods. The BSL6D simulation code addresses challenges in high-performance computing, and exposes inter-node hardware bottlenecks. A physical model with net-zero charge transport is validated against ion Bernstein wave theory.},
	keywords = {},
	note = {},
	url = {https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/1762920},
	doi = {},
}

title: Uncertainty quantification in three-dimensional plasma equilibrium reconstruction
author: Robert Köberl
bibtex:
@phdthesis{dissertation,
	author = {Köberl, Robert},
	title = {Uncertainty quantification in three-dimensional plasma equilibrium reconstruction},
	year = {2025},
	school = {Technische Universität München},
	pages = {153},
	language = {en},
	abstract = {The ideal magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) equilibrium is central to analyzing magnetically confined plasmas. Equilibrium reconstruction involves inferring an MHD equilibrium from experimental data. For 3D geometries, such as stellarators, this can be computationally intensive. This work explores probabilistic approaches for uncertainty quantification in equilibrium reconstruction using data-driven methods like dimensionality reduction and surrogate modeling. This enables sample-based uncertainty estimates on equilibrium quantities while reducing computational costs.},
	keywords = {Plasma Physics;Ideal Magnetohydrodynamics;Equilibrium Reconstruction;Uncertainty Quantification;Bayesian Inference;Surrogate Modeling;Dimensionality Reduction},
	note = {},
	url = {https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/1763047},
	doi = {},
}

title: Structure-Preserving Neural Networks for Scientific Computing
author: Benedikt Brantner
bibtex:
@phdthesis{dissertation,
	author = {Brantner, Benedikt},
	title = {Geometric Machine Learning},
	year = {2025},
	school = {Technische Universität München},
	pages = {306},
	language = {en},
	abstract = {In this dissertation we propose an array of novel neural network architectures and optimization algorithms to treat problems in scientific computing, especially reduced order modeling. All the new architectures are structure-preserving (symplectic, volume-preserving, ...) in some sense and we show how these structure-preserving properties can improve the application of neural networks to problems from scientific computing.},
	keywords = {},
	note = {},
	url = {https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/1754855},
	doi = {},
}

title: Beyond gyrokinetic theory - Excitation of high-frequency turbulence in 6D Vlasov simulations of magnetized plasmas with steep temperature and density gradients
author: Mario Räth
bibtex:
@phdthesis{dissertation,
	author = {Räth, Mario},
	title = {Beyond gyrokinetic theory},
	year = {2023},
	school = {Technische Universität München},
	pages = {161},
	language = {en},
	abstract = {In this work, the stability of ion Bernstein waves (IBWs) is extensively studied in various conditions. Analytical investigations of the dispersion relation are combined with numerical simulations with a novel 6D kinetic Vlasov code. In addition, the energy fluxes induced by such waves have been investigated. The analysis shows the relevance of high-frequency waves in regimes of steep gradients, commonly found in the plasma edge of fusion devices.},
	keywords = {plasma physics, turbulence, fusion, edge physics, ion bernstein waves},
	note = {},
	url = {https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/1703830},
	doi = {},
}

title: Reduced basis methods for problems with moving features
author: Tobias Blickhan
bibtex:
@phdthesis{dissertation,
	author = {Blickhan, Tobias Michael},
	title = {Reduced basis methods for problems with moving features},
	year = {2024},
	school = {Technische Universität München},
	pages = {145},
	language = {en},
	abstract = {This work deals with reduced order methods for the solution of parametrized partial differential equations with moving features. Solutions of these class of equations are well known to be hard to approximate using linear reduced basis methods. We utilize results from optimal transportation theory to extend these methods. In particular, we present a novel registration method that is based on aligning solution features using an approximation of optimal transport maps.},
	keywords = {parameterized partial differential equation, model order reduction, snapshot registration, optimal transport, transport-dominated problems},
	note = {},
	url = {https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/1726153},
	doi = {},
}

title: Geometric Particle-in-Cell Methods on Mapped Grids
author: Benedikt Perse
bibtex:
@phdthesis{dissertation,
	author = {Perse, Benedikt},
	title = {Geometric Particle-in-Cell Methods on Mapped Grids},
	year = {2021},
	school = {Technische Universität München},
	pages = {173},
	language = {en},
	abstract = {The geometric electromagnetic particle-in-cell (GEMPIC) framework provides the foundation for Vlasov-Maxwell solvers that preserve at the discrete level the non-canonical Hamiltonian structure. Preserving the structure of the kinetic equations enables stable numerical methods for long time simulations. In this dissertation, the GEMPIC framework is extended to curvilinear coordinates and perfect conductor boundary conditions. Several semi-implicit time integrators based either on a Hamiltonian splitting or on an antisymmetric splitting of the Poisson matrix are discussed and assessed regarding their conservation properties and computational efficiency.},
	keywords = {},
	note = {},
	url = {https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/1611205},
	doi = {},
}
